prompt: Complete: 'Ho comprato ____ in libreria.' [some books] (qualche)

must_not_include.note: Qualche always takes the SINGULAR noun, even though the meaning is plural: qualche libro = some books.
Qualche never inflects.

explanation: I bought some books at the bookshop: Ho comprato qualche libro in libreria. English puts the plural on the noun (books), and Italian does not: qualche is invariable and the noun after it stays singular, with qualche itself carrying the plural meaning. So qualche libro is already 'some books'. If you want the plural noun, you need the other word for it: alcuni libri.

prompt: Which is correct?
explanation: Ho letto qualche libro means 'I read some books.' Qualche is invariable, so it never becomes qualchi; and the noun it introduces stays singular however plural the meaning is. Reading 'qualche libro' as 'one book' is the trap: the plural lives in qualche, not on the noun.
examiner_note: Index-scored; the markpoint is retained for bucket attribution. Choices are safe as substrings: 'Ho letto qualche libri' does not contain 'Ho letto qualche libro' (libro/libri diverge at the final character).

prompt: Complete: 'Ho ____ per il fine settimana.' [a few ideas] (qualche)

must_not_include.note: The noun after qualche stays singular: qualche idea = a few ideas.

explanation: I have a few ideas for the weekend: Ho qualche idea per il fine settimana. Qualche is invariable: it does not change for gender either, so it is qualche idea just as it is qualche libro. Only the noun's own form matters, and it stays singular.

prompt: Complete: '____ è arrivato in ritardo stamattina.' [a few students] (qualche)

must_not_include.note: Qualche takes the singular noun: qualche studente, even meaning several.

explanation: A few students arrived late this morning: Qualche studente è arrivato in ritardo stamattina. Notice the verb: è arrivato, singular. Qualche + singular noun is grammatically singular all the way through the sentence, even though the meaning is plural. The verb in the prompt is your clue to the shape the subject has to take.

prompt: Complete: 'C'è ____ da risolvere.' [a few problems] (qualche)

must_not_include.note: Qualche takes the singular: qualche problema.
Problema is masculine but its singular ends in -a: il problema, i problemi.

explanation: There are a few problems to sort out: C'è qualche problema da risolvere. Two things at once here. Qualche wants the singular, so not problemi. And the singular is problema, not problemo: problema is one of a small set of masculine nouns ending in -a (il problema, il programma, il sistema), which take -i in the plural like any other masculine noun. Notice the verb too: c'è, singular, because qualche + singular noun is singular all the way through.
examiner_note: Deliberate exception-density item (house technique 2): the masculine -a noun sits inside an otherwise regular qualche set, so the learner cannot autopilot on 'singular = drop the -i'.

prompt: Complete: 'Hai ____ prima di cominciare?' [any questions] (qualche)

must_not_include.note: The noun after qualche stays singular: qualche domanda.

explanation: Do you have any questions before we start? Hai qualche domanda prima di cominciare? English 'any questions' is plural; Italian says qualche domanda, singular. This is the everyday way to ask it, and getting the singular right is what makes it sound native.

prompt: Complete: 'Vorrei restare ancora ____.' [a few days] (qualche)

must_not_include.note: Qualche takes the singular: qualche giorno = a few days.

explanation: I'd like to stay a few more days: Vorrei restare ancora qualche giorno. Qualche giorno is one of the phrases you will hear constantly, and it is always singular. Compare alcuni giorni, which means the same and takes the plural.

prompt: Complete: 'Qualche amico ____ già arrivato.' (essere)

must_not_include.note: Qualche + singular noun is grammatically singular, so the verb is singular too: qualche amico e arrivato.

explanation: Some friends have already arrived: Qualche amico e già arrivato. The meaning is plural but the grammar is singular throughout: qualche forces a singular noun, and that singular noun then forces a singular verb. This is where the calque usually shows up, because the English behind it is plural and pulls you toward sono.
examiner_note: Criterion 19 checked, no flag: the accent-stripped twin 'e' (the conjunction) is not a plausible alternative ANSWER at a verb blank, which is the DECISIONS 2026-07-14 precedent for essere/dare present items. Fold-rescue plus an orthography miss is the right verdict. Both spellings are listed anyway so the strict pass catches the accented form first.

prompt: Complete: '____ ragazze sono arrivate presto.' [some]

any_phrases.note: Delle ragazze is correct Italian, but it is the partitive rather than the word this item is practising. Alcune is what was wanted here.

must_not_include.note: Alcuni is the masculine plural; ragazze is feminine, so it is alcune.
Qualche would need a singular noun (qualche ragazza), but the sentence already has ragazze sono arrivate.

explanation: Some girls arrived early: Alcune ragazze sono arrivate presto. Alcuni is the plural way of saying some, and unlike qualche it agrees: alcuni with masculine nouns, alcune with feminine ones. Ragazze is feminine plural, so alcune. The noun and the verb are already plural in the sentence, which is your signal that this is the alcuni family rather than qualche.
examiner_note: Rev 17(iv) 0.9-for-dodges: 'delle ragazze' is correct Italian that sidesteps the drilled word. Rev 20(i) checked -- it is NOT this bucket's named common_miss (that is 'alcuni with a feminine noun'), so the specific-diagnosis-outranks-the-general-rule carve-out does not apply and the default 0.9 stands. 'qualche' IS guarded rather than graded: the frame (plural noun + plural verb already present) makes it wrong, not merely off-pattern.

prompt: Complete: 'Ho letto ____ libri molto interessanti.' [some]

must_not_include.note: Libri is masculine plural, so alcuni.
Qualche would need the singular: qualche libro. The sentence already has libri.

explanation: I read some very interesting books: Ho letto alcuni libri molto interessanti. Alcuni agrees with what follows, and libri is masculine plural, so alcuni. This is the sentence that shows the whole contrast: alcuni libri and qualche libro mean the same thing, and only one of them lets you keep the plural noun.

prompt: Complete: 'Ho comprato ____ mele al mercato.' [some]

must_not_include.note: Mele is feminine plural, so alcune.

explanation: I bought some apples at the market: Ho comprato alcune mele al mercato. Mele is feminine plural, so the agreement gives alcune. The rule is ordinary adjective agreement; the only thing that makes it slip is that English 'some' gives you no gender to copy.

prompt: Say the same thing using alcuni: 'Ho letto qualche libro.' -> 'Ho letto ____.'

must_not_include.note: Alcuni takes the PLURAL noun: alcuni libri. That is the whole difference from qualche.
Libri is masculine, so alcuni.
Alcuni dei libri means 'some OF the books', a different sentence: it points at a known set.

explanation: Both sentences mean 'I read some books.' Ho letto qualche libro and Ho letto alcuni libri are interchangeable here; what changes is the machinery. Qualche is invariable and forces the singular; alcuni agrees and takes the plural. Swapping one for the other means moving the plural off the quantifier and onto the noun, which is why 'alcuni libro' is the giveaway error.
examiner_note: The instruction pins alcuni, so a qualche answer is non-compliance rather than a dodge and collects no 0.9.

prompt: Complete: '____ studenti e studentesse hanno protestato.' [some]

must_not_include.note: A mixed group of studenti e studentesse takes the masculine plural: alcuni.

explanation: Some students, male and female, protested: Alcuni studenti e studentesse hanno protestato. When a group is mixed, Italian agreement defaults to the masculine plural, however many women are in it. So alcuni, not alcune, even though studentesse is sitting right there. This is the same mixed-gender plural rule that runs through the whole adjective system.

prompt: Complete: '____ dicono che il film è bello, altri no.' [some people]

must_not_include.note: With no noun to agree with, the default for people in general is the masculine plural: alcuni.
Qualche cannot stand alone; it always needs a noun after it.

explanation: Some say the film is good, others don't: Alcuni dicono che il film è bello, altri no. Here alcuni stands on its own with no noun after it, meaning 'some people'. The pairing with altri is the giveaway that a bare pronoun is wanted. Qualche can never do this: it always needs a noun to lean on.
examiner_note: Scope note for the coverage doc: the leaf's description covers alcuni ADJECTIVALLY ('alcuni ragazzi, alcune ragazze') and says nothing about standalone pronoun use. Authored here because the pronoun is common at B1 and the leaf is its only plausible home; flagged to Architecture rather than assumed.

prompt: Complete: 'Vado in palestra ____.' [every day] (ogni)

must_not_include.note: Ogni is invariable and always takes a SINGULAR noun: ogni giorno.
Ogni takes no article. Compare tutti I giorni, which needs one.

explanation: I go to the gym every day: Vado in palestra ogni giorno. Ogni is invariable, and the noun after it is always singular, so ogni giorno and never ogni giorni. Italian has a second way of saying the same thing, tutti i giorni, and that one does take the plural and an article: same meaning, different machinery.

prompt: Complete: '____ che lo vedo, mi saluta.' [every time] (ogni)

must_not_include.note: Ogni takes the singular: ogni volta.

explanation: Every time I see him, he says hello: Ogni volta che lo vedo, mi saluta. Ogni volta is fixed and singular, like ogni giorno. Ogni is invariable, so it does not shift for the feminine volta either.

prompt: Complete: 'Ci vediamo ____ in biblioteca.' [every Monday] (ogni)
explanation: We see each other every Monday at the library: Ci vediamo ogni lunedì in biblioteca. Days of the week take no article after ogni, and lunedì does not change in the plural anyway: its final stressed vowel makes it invariable, so i lunedì and ogni lunedì are spelled identically. The accent is not optional, though: it marks where the stress falls.
examiner_note: Criterion 19 re-checked AFTER the cue decision, per Rev 23. No flag: 'lunedi' unaccented is the same word misspelled, not a plausible alternative ANSWER to this prompt, so the fold-rescue plus an orthography miss is the correct verdict (criterion 19's own 'ando for ando' class). Both spellings listed so the strict pass hits the accented form first and the fold only catches the slip.

prompt: Say the same thing using ogni: 'Vado al mercato tutti i giorni.' -> 'Vado al mercato ____.'

must_not_include.note: Moving to ogni means moving to the singular: ogni giorno.
Ogni never takes an article; that was tutti's.

explanation: Both sentences mean 'I go to the market every day.' Tutti i giorni and ogni giorno are interchangeable, and converting between them means changing three things at once: tutti becomes ogni, the article disappears, and the noun goes singular. Dropping only the article is the usual half-conversion.
examiner_note: The instruction pins ogni, so a tutti-i answer is non-compliance, not a dodge; no 0.9.

prompt: Which is correct?
explanation: Ogni settimana means 'every week.' Ogni is invariable, so there is no ogne and no ogni-plus-plural: the noun after it stays singular whatever the gender, and no article comes between them.
examiner_note: Index-scored, markpoint retained for attribution. Containment checked on the normalised forms: 'ogni settimana' is not a substring of any distractor ('ogni settimane' diverges at the final character).

prompt: Complete: '____ deve portare un documento d'identità.' [every student] (ogni)

must_not_include.note: Ogni takes the singular: ogni studente.

explanation: Every student must bring an identity document: Ogni studente deve portare un documento d'identità. The verb deve is singular, which is your confirmation: ogni + singular noun is singular all through, exactly like qualche.

prompt: Complete: 'Ho lavorato ____.' [all day] (tutto)

must_not_include.note: Tutto keeps the article after it: tutto IL giorno.
Tutti i giorni means 'every day', not 'all day'.
The article stays here too: tutta LA giornata.

explanation: I worked all day: Ho lavorato tutto il giorno. The thing English speakers drop is the article: Italian says tutto IL giorno, never tutto giorno. Tutto sits in front of the whole article-plus-noun block rather than replacing the article, which is why both words survive. Tutta la giornata says the same thing with a different noun and is equally good.
examiner_note: 'tutta la giornata' was a must_not_include at first draft and is now a full-credit positive: it is correct Italian that uses the pattern under test, so guarding it would have been a false diagnostic (criterion 18's third direction). The item tests whether the article survives, and both accepted phrases test it.

prompt: Complete: '____ è venuta alla festa.' [the whole class] (tutto)

must_not_include.note: The article stays: tutta LA classe.
Tutto agrees with the noun: classe is feminine singular, so tutta.
That is 'all the classes', a different meaning.

explanation: The whole class came to the party: Tutta la classe è venuta alla festa. Two jobs in one answer. Tutto agrees like an ordinary adjective, and classe is feminine singular, so tutta. Then the article stays put: tutta LA classe.

prompt: Complete: 'Vado in palestra ____.' [every day] (tutto)

must_not_include.note: The article stays: tutti I giorni.
Giorni is masculine plural, so tutti i.
Ogni giorno means the same, but the item is practising the tutti construction.

explanation: I go to the gym every day: Vado in palestra tutti i giorni. This is the plural twin of ogni giorno, and it needs both the plural noun and the article: tutti I giorni. Notice how differently the two work for the same meaning: ogni takes a bare singular, tutti takes an article plus a plural.

prompt: Complete: 'Studio ____ dopo cena.' [every evening] (tutto)

must_not_include.note: The article stays: tutte LE sere.
Sere is feminine plural (la sera, le sere), so tutte le.
Tutte is plural, so the noun is plural too: le sere.

explanation: I study every evening after dinner: Studio tutte le sere dopo cena. Sera is feminine, so the plural is le sere and tutto agrees as tutte. The article survives here exactly as it does in tutti i giorni.

prompt: Which is correct for 'I waited all morning'?
explanation: Ho aspettato tutta la mattina means 'I waited all morning.' Tutto agrees with mattina (feminine singular, so tutta) and the article stays between them. Dropping the article is the single most common way this phrase goes wrong, because English gives you nothing to copy it from.
examiner_note: Index-scored. Containment checked on the normalised strings: the correct choice is not a substring of any distractor, and no distractor is a substring of it ('tutta mattina' is not inside 'tutta la mattina' -- the article intervenes).

prompt: Complete: 'Ho salutato ____ prima di uscire.' [all the students, a mixed group] (tutto)

must_not_include.note: The article stays after tutti: tutti GLI studenti.

markpoints.label: Masculine plural article before s + consonant

must_not_include.note: Studenti begins with s + consonant, so the article is gli, not i (the same rule as lo studente).

explanation: I said goodbye to all the students before leaving: Ho salutato tutti gli studenti prima di uscire. Two separate skills land in this one answer. Tutto keeps the article after it, which gives tutti + article + studenti; and the article itself has to be chosen, where studenti starts with s + consonant and so takes gli rather than i, just as lo studente does in the singular. A mixed group defaults to the masculine plural.
examiner_note: Criterion 8: two markpoints because two distinct skills combine. A learner who keeps the article but picks 'i' scores 1/2 with the miss correctly attributed to the article tree, not to mine. Cross-tree citation of article.definite.forms per the DECISIONS 2026-07-14 ruling that tutto + article lives here and cites the article tree.

prompt: Complete: 'Ha piovuto ____.' [the whole week] (tutto)

must_not_include.note: The article stays: tutta LA settimana.
Settimana is feminine singular, so tutta.
That is 'every week', a different meaning.

explanation: It rained the whole week: Ha piovuto tutta la settimana. Settimana is feminine singular, so tutto agrees as tutta, and the article stays. Note the meaning split against tutte le settimane, which would be 'every week' rather than 'the whole of one week'.

prompt: Complete: 'C'è ____ da vedere in questa città?' [something beautiful]

must_not_include.note: An adjective after qualcosa needs DI: qualcosa di bello.
After qualcosa the adjective is always masculine singular, whatever the thing turns out to be.
Qualcuno is for people; a thing to see is qualcosa.

explanation: Is there something beautiful to see in this city? C'è qualcosa di bello da vedere in questa città? Italian will not let an adjective sit straight after qualcosa: it needs di as a joint, giving qualcosa di bello. And the adjective is always masculine singular there, because qualcosa has no gender of its own to hand over.

prompt: Complete: 'Ho fame. Vorrei ____.' [something to eat]

must_not_include.note: An infinitive after qualcosa needs DA: qualcosa da mangiare.
Di introduces an adjective (qualcosa di buono); DA introduces an infinitive (qualcosa da mangiare).

explanation: I'm hungry. I'd like something to eat: Ho fame. Vorrei qualcosa da mangiare. Where an adjective needs di, a verb needs da: qualcosa DA mangiare. The pair is worth learning together, because the two little words are not interchangeable and choosing between them is the whole skill: qualcosa di buono da mangiare uses both at once.

prompt: Complete: '____ ha telefonato mentre eri fuori.' [somebody]

must_not_include.note: A caller is a person, so qualcuno.
Qualcheduno exists but is archaic; the everyday word is qualcuno.

explanation: Somebody phoned while you were out: Qualcuno ha telefonato mentre eri fuori. The split is simple and absolute: qualcuno for people, qualcosa for things. Nothing in the sentence marks it except the meaning, which is why the verb telefonare is doing the work here.

prompt: Complete: 'Qualcuno ____ lasciato un messaggio per te.' (avere)

must_not_include.note: Qualcuno is grammatically singular however many people it turns out to mean: qualcuno ha.

explanation: Somebody left a message for you: Qualcuno ha lasciato un messaggio per te. Qualcuno is always singular, even when you suspect several people were involved. English 'somebody' behaves the same way, so this one is usually lost to speed rather than to the rule.
examiner_note: Criterion 18, rightward: 'ha' sits inside 'hanno' and 'hai', so an unanchored positive would credit the exact wrong answer this item exists to catch. Every phrase carries match_at 'word'. This is the pluperfect era/eravamo shape.

prompt: Complete: 'Ho sentito ____ alla radio stamattina.' [something strange]

must_not_include.note: An adjective after qualcosa needs DI.
The adjective after qualcosa is fixed masculine singular: di strano.

explanation: I heard something strange on the radio this morning: Ho sentito qualcosa di strano alla radio stamattina. Di again, and again the adjective is masculine singular. It stays masculine even when the thing you have in mind is feminine, because you are agreeing with qualcosa itself and qualcosa has no gender to give.

prompt: Which is correct for 'I saw something beautiful'?
explanation: Ho visto qualcosa di bello means 'I saw something beautiful.' Three things have to be right at once: qualcosa rather than qualcuno because it is a thing, di rather than nothing because an adjective is coming, and bello rather than bella because the adjective after qualcosa is always masculine singular.
examiner_note: Index-scored. The three distractors are the three separate errors the short items test individually; this one checks the learner can hold all three at once.

prompt: Complete: 'Hai ____ stasera o sei libero?' [something to do]

must_not_include.note: An infinitive after qualcosa needs DA.
DA introduces an infinitive; di introduces an adjective.

explanation: Have you got something to do this evening, or are you free? Hai qualcosa da fare stasera o sei libero? Qualcosa da fare is a set phrase you will use constantly. The da is doing the same job as the 'to' in English 'to do', which makes this the easier half of the di/da pair to remember.

prompt: Complete: 'C'è ____ alla porta.' [somebody]

must_not_include.note: Somebody at the door is a person: qualcuno.
Nessuno would mean the opposite, and would need non: non c'e nessuno.

explanation: There's somebody at the door: C'è qualcuno alla porta. Qualcuno for people, qualcosa for things, and the choice rests entirely on meaning. Watch the negative twin too: the opposite of this sentence is non c'è nessuno, where the non is compulsory.

prompt: Complete: '____! Ci penso io.' [no problem] (nessuno)

must_not_include.note: Before a normal consonant nessuno drops to nessun, exactly as uno drops to un: un problema, nessun problema.
Problema is masculine despite the -a ending: un problema, nessun problema.
Nessuno never goes plural.

explanation: No problem! I'll take care of it: Nessun problema! Ci penso io. When nessuno sits in front of a noun it behaves exactly like the indefinite article uno, and takes the same endings: un problema gives nessun problema, uno zaino gives nessuno zaino, una casa gives nessuna casa. Learn the article and you already know this. Nessun problema is worth having by heart: it is the everyday 'no worries'.

prompt: Complete: 'Non ho ____ qui a Roma.' [no friend] (nessuno)

must_not_include.note: Before a vowel the masculine is nessun, like un amico.
Amico is masculine: nessun amico.
Nessuno never goes plural.

explanation: I have no friends here in Rome: Non ho nessun amico qui a Roma. Amico starts with a vowel, and the masculine article there is un amico, so nessuno follows it to nessun amico. Note the non earlier in the sentence: Italian wants both halves of the negative, and that pairing is the negation topic's rule rather than this one's.

prompt: Complete: 'Non ho portato ____ in gita.' [no rucksack] (nessuno)

must_not_include.note: Words beginning with z take the long form: uno zaino, nessuno zaino.

explanation: I didn't bring a rucksack on the trip: Non ho portato nessuno zaino in gita. Zaino starts with z, and z is one of the sounds that forces the long article: uno zaino, never un zaino. Nessuno copies it exactly, so the full form survives: nessuno zaino. This is where the parallel with the article earns its keep.

prompt: Complete: 'Non c'è ____ in questa strada.' [no house] (nessuno)

must_not_include.note: Casa is feminine and starts with a consonant: una casa, nessuna casa.

explanation: There isn't a single house on this street: Non c'è nessuna casa in questa strada. Casa is feminine and begins with a consonant, so the article is una casa and nessuno follows to nessuna casa. The feminine keeps its full form in front of a consonant, exactly as una does.

prompt: Complete: 'Non ho ____ di dove sia andato.' [no idea] (nessuno)

must_not_include.note: Nessuno never goes plural.

explanation: I have no idea where he's gone: Non ho nessun'idea di dove sia andato. Idea is feminine and starts with a vowel, which is where the article elides: un'idea, with the apostrophe. Nessuno does the same, giving nessun'idea. You will also hear nessuna idea, and it is accepted; the elided form is the one that matches the article pattern the rest of this topic runs on.
examiner_note: AUTHORING DECISION, flagged in the coverage doc. The apostrophe here is NOT deterministically gradable: norm() folds an apostrophe to a space and collapses whitespace, so norm("nessun'idea") and norm("nessun idea") are BOTH "nessun idea", character-identical. Verified against housing/js/norm.js line 106, not assumed. This is the article tree's ratified un'/un position. So the item does not pretend to test the apostrophe: it tests that the learner reaches for nessuno and agrees it feminine, accepts both attested forms at full credit, and teaches the elision in the explanation. The apostrophe itself is tested in translation (trans_ind_en_it_nn_04), where the AI marker can see the glyph.

prompt: Complete: '____ studente ha superato la prova.' [no student] (nessuno)

must_not_include.note: Studente begins with s + consonant, which takes the long form: uno studente, nessuno studente.

explanation: Not one student passed the test: Nessuno studente ha superato la prova. Studente begins with s followed by another consonant, and that combination forces the long article: uno studente. Nessuno keeps its full form to match. Note the verb: ha superato, singular, because nessuno + singular noun is singular throughout.
examiner_note: OIC seed sentence, verbatim from their exercise (nessuno as adjective + singular noun).

prompt: Complete: 'Non ho visto ____ alla festa.' [nobody]

must_not_include.note: Niente is for things; for people it is nessuno.
Nulla is for things; for people it is nessuno.
With no noun to agree with, the pronoun is just nessuno.

explanation: I didn't see anybody at the party: Non ho visto nessuno alla festa. Standing on its own as a pronoun, nessuno takes no endings at all: it is simply nessuno for anyone of any gender. The people-versus-things split is the same one qualcuno and qualcosa make, just on the negative side.

prompt: Complete: 'Non ho detto ____ a nessuno.' [nothing]

must_not_include.note: Nessuno is for people; for things it is niente or nulla.
Qualcosa is the positive twin; the sentence is negative.

explanation: I didn't say anything to anybody: Non ho detto niente a nessuno. Niente and nulla are interchangeable here and both are correct; nulla feels a shade more formal. Notice the sentence carries three negatives at once (non, niente, nessuno) and stays perfectly ordinary Italian: piling them up is the norm, not an error.

prompt: Which is correct?
explanation: Non ho nessun dubbio means 'I have no doubt.' Nessuno never goes plural: there is no nessuni and no nessune, however plural the English feels. And before a plain consonant it drops to nessun, following un dubbio.
examiner_note: Index-scored. Containment audited on normalised strings: the correct choice is not a substring of any distractor and vice versa.

prompt: Complete: 'Ognuno di noi ____ un compito diverso.' (avere)

must_not_include.note: Ognuno is singular, however plural 'di noi' feels: ognuno di noi HA.
The subject is ognuno, not noi: the verb answers to ognuno and stays third-person singular.

explanation: Each of us has a different task: Ognuno di noi ha un compito diverso. The pull toward the plural is strong because di noi is sitting right next to the verb, but noi is not the subject: ognuno is, and it is singular. Italian agrees with the head of the phrase, so ognuno di noi ha, never hanno.
examiner_note: Criterion 18, rightward: 'ha' sits inside both 'hanno' and 'abbiamo'... (checked: 'abbiamo' does NOT contain 'ha' -- a,b,b,i,a,m,o. 'hanno' DOES, at index 0). Since any_phrases wins BEFORE must_not_include is ever reached, an unanchored 'ha' would have credited 'hanno' outright -- the item's whole point, silently inverted. match_at 'word' on every phrase. This is the pluperfect era/eravamo bug in a different paradigm.

prompt: Complete: '____ ha portato qualcosa da mangiare.' [everybody, one by one]

must_not_include.note: Ogni cannot stand alone; it always needs a noun. The pronoun is ognuno.
Tutti would take a plural verb (tutti hanno portato); the verb here is singular.

explanation: Everybody brought something to eat: Ognuno ha portato qualcosa da mangiare. Ognuno is the pronoun and stands alone; ogni is the adjective and always needs a noun behind it. The singular verb ha is your confirmation. Tutti would say much the same thing but would need a plural verb: tutti hanno portato.

prompt: Complete, in formal written register: '____ deve presentare la domanda entro venerdì.' [each candidate] (ciascuno)

must_not_include.note: Before a plain consonant ciascuno drops to ciascun, exactly like uno and nessuno.
Ciascuno never goes plural.

explanation: Each candidate must submit the application by Friday: Ciascun candidato deve presentare la domanda entro venerdì. Ciascuno takes the same endings as uno and nessuno when it sits in front of a noun: un candidato gives ciascun candidato. Register matters here: ciascuno leans formal and belongs in a notice like this one, where ognuno would sound conversational.
examiner_note: Criterion 11: the register is stated in the prompt, because ciascuno vs ognuno is a register call and without the cue the item would test register-detection rather than the form.

prompt: Complete: 'Ciascuno di voi ____ ricevere un attestato.' (potere)

must_not_include.note: Ciascuno is singular however plural 'di voi' feels: ciascuno di voi PUO.
The subject is ciascuno, not voi: the verb stays third-person singular.

explanation: Each of you can receive a certificate: Ciascuno di voi può ricevere un attestato. Ciascuno behaves exactly like ognuno: it is the subject, di voi is only a modifier, and the verb stays singular. The plural pronoun sitting next to the verb is the trap in both.
examiner_note: Criterion 19 checked, no flag: 'puo' unaccented is the same form misspelled, not a plausible alternative ANSWER to this prompt (there is no unaccented word 'puo' competing for the slot), so the fold-rescue plus an orthography miss is correct. Both spellings listed so the strict pass hits the accented one first.

prompt: Which is correct?
explanation: Ognuno ha la sua opinione means 'Everyone has their own opinion.' Three traps in one line: ogni cannot stand without a noun, ognuno has no plural, and ognuno takes a singular verb. Ognuno ha is the only combination that survives all three.
examiner_note: Index-scored. Containment audited: the correct choice shares its opening with distractor 4 ('Ognuno ha...' vs 'Ognuno hanno...') but is not a substring of it, since the strings diverge at 'ha ' vs 'hanno'.

prompt: Complete, in formal written register: 'A ____ studente è stato assegnato un tutor.' [each] (ciascuno)

must_not_include.note: Studente begins with s + consonant, so the long form survives: ciascuno studente, like uno studente.

explanation: Each student has been assigned a tutor: A ciascuno studente è stato assegnato un tutor. Studente begins with s + consonant, which forces the long form of the article (uno studente), and ciascuno follows it exactly as nessuno does. Same phonology, three different words: uno, nessuno, ciascuno.
examiner_note: Same-surface, opposite-answer pairing with ind_oc_03 (house technique 3): ciascun candidato there, ciascuno studente here. The conditioning factor is the noun's opening sound, and the pair teaches it better than either item alone.

prompt: Complete: '____ faccia, la fa sempre con grande impegno.' [whatever he does]

must_not_include.note: Qualsiasi is invariable and the noun after it stays singular: qualsiasi cosa.
Qualunque never inflects.

explanation: Whatever he does, he always does it with great commitment: Qualsiasi cosa faccia, la fa sempre con grande impegno. Qualunque and qualsiasi are interchangeable and both invariable: no qualunqui, no qualsiasi-plural, and the noun after them stays singular. The verb faccia in the prompt is subjunctive, which is what these words pull in when they open a subordinate clause.
examiner_note: OIC seed sentence, verbatim. The subjunctive is SUPPLIED per the dispatch's two-sided contract: the mood-choice drill belongs to tense_choice, and this item tests the indefinite word itself.

prompt: Complete: 'Puoi chiamarmi a ____ del giorno o della notte.' [any hour]

must_not_include.note: The noun after qualsiasi stays singular: qualsiasi ora.

explanation: You can call me at any hour of the day or night: Puoi chiamarmi a qualsiasi ora del giorno o della notte. Like qualche and ogni, these words take a singular noun and no article. Qualsiasi ora means any hour at all, whichever you pick, which is the free-choice sense: not 'every hour' but 'take your pick'.

prompt: Complete: 'Non è un vino speciale, è un vino ____.' [ordinary, nothing special]

must_not_include.note: Ogni cannot go after a noun, and would mean 'every'.

explanation: It isn't a special wine, it's just an ordinary wine: Non è un vino speciale, è un vino qualunque. Put after the noun, qualunque changes meaning: it stops being 'any' and turns dismissive, meaning run-of-the-mill. Un vino qualunque is a wine like any other. Before the noun it would be free choice; after it, a shrug.
examiner_note: Criterion 11: the post-nominal reading is a meaning/register shift, so the prompt states the sense wanted ('ordinary, nothing special') rather than leaving the learner to guess which of the two positions is being drilled.

prompt: Complete: '____ persona può iscriversi, non importa l'età.' [any]

must_not_include.note: Chiunque is a pronoun and can never sit in front of a noun. Before a noun it must be qualunque or qualsiasi.
Ognuno is a pronoun too, and cannot stand before a noun.

explanation: Any person can enrol, whatever their age: Qualunque persona può iscriversi, non importa l'età. This is the sentence that separates the two free-choice words. Qualunque and qualsiasi are adjectives, so they can stand in front of persona. Chiunque means almost the same but is a pronoun only: it can replace the whole phrase (Chiunque può iscriversi) but can never lead a noun.
examiner_note: Frame deliberately built so the blank is the determiner ALONE, with 'persona' fixed in the prompt. Criterion 18, correct-inside-guard: had the blank swallowed the noun, the correct 'qualunque persona' would have nested at clean word boundaries inside nothing -- but the WRONG 'chiunque persona' would have been unreachable in the mirror item, because 'chiunque' alone is a legitimate answer to an open subject slot. Pinning the noun in the prompt makes both the positive and the guard single words in a fixed frame, so anchoring is sufficient and no additive error escapes.

prompt: Which is correct?
explanation: Qualsiasi studente può partecipare means 'Any student can take part.' Qualsiasi never inflects, so there is no qualsiasii and no qualunqui; and the noun after it stays singular, which then keeps the verb singular too.
examiner_note: Index-scored. Containment audited on the normalised forms: 'Qualsiasi studente puo partecipare' is NOT a substring of 'Qualsiasii studente puo partecipare' -- the doubled i breaks the run at the word boundary.

prompt: Complete: '____ tu dica, non cambierò idea.' [whatever you say]
explanation: Whatever you say, I won't change my mind: Qualunque cosa tu dica, non cambierò idea. English 'whatever' is one word; Italian needs two, qualunque cosa, with cosa carrying the noun slot the adjective requires. The subjunctive dica in the prompt is what these words attract in a subordinate clause.
examiner_note: Subjunctive supplied per the two-sided contract; see the free_choice_mood_seam thread.

prompt: Complete: '____ sia, digli che non ci sono.' [whoever it is]

must_not_include.note: Qualunque is an adjective and needs a noun; standing alone for a person it is chiunque.

explanation: Whoever it is, tell him I'm not here: Chiunque sia, digli che non ci sono. Chiunque stands entirely alone: it means 'whoever' and never leans on a noun. That is the one thing separating it from qualunque, which needs one. The subjunctive sia in the prompt is what chiunque pulls in when it opens a clause like this.
examiner_note: OIC seed sentence, verbatim. Subjunctive supplied per the two-sided contract. A 'Chiunque persona' guard was authored here at first draft and REMOVED by the marker replica: the correct answer nests inside it at clean word boundaries, so the positive fires first and the error is credited outright -- criterion 18's correct-inside-guard case, unreachable by anchoring because the error adds a word. §18 forbids leaving a guard that implies a catch it cannot make. The error is caught by ind_chi_02 (index-scored) and trans_ind_en_it_chi_01 (AI marker).

prompt: Complete: '____ persona in possesso del biglietto può entrare.' [any]

must_not_include.note: Chiunque is a pronoun: it cannot stand in front of persona. Before a noun you need qualsiasi or qualunque.

explanation: Any person holding a ticket may enter: Qualsiasi persona in possesso del biglietto può entrare. The test is whether chiunque can lead a noun, and it cannot: it is a pronoun and nothing else. Chiunque in possesso del biglietto può entrare would be perfectly good Italian -- but only because chiunque has replaced the whole noun phrase, not because it has modified it.
examiner_note: MCQ by necessity, not by taste. This is the pronoun-vs-adjective diagnostic and it is UNREACHABLE by the substring engine in short form: at an open subject slot the wrong 'chiunque persona' contains the correct 'chiunque' at clean word boundaries, so the positive fires first and credits the error outright -- criterion 18's correct-inside-guard case, which the brief rules must go to MCQ or the AI strand rather than be left as a guard implying a catch. Index-scoring reaches it; anchoring cannot.

prompt: Complete: '____ può partecipare al concorso, senza limiti di età.' [anyone]

must_not_include.note: Standing alone with no noun, it must be chiunque.
Ognuno means 'each one' of a known group; chiunque means 'anyone at all'.

explanation: Anyone can enter the competition, with no age limit: Chiunque può partecipare al concorso, senza limiti di età. Here chiunque is the whole subject on its own, which is what it does best. The contrast with ognuno is worth holding: ognuno picks out each member of a group you already have in mind, while chiunque throws the door open to anybody at all.

prompt: Complete using ONE of: qualunque, chiunque, ciascuno, nessuno. '____ sia stato, ha fatto un ottimo lavoro.' [whoever it was]

must_not_include.note: Qualunque needs a noun after it; alone, for a person, it is chiunque.
Ciascuno picks out each member of a known group; this sentence has no group, just an unknown someone.
Nessuno is negative and would contradict 'ha fatto un ottimo lavoro'.

explanation: Whoever it was, they did an excellent job: Chiunque sia stato, ha fatto un ottimo lavoro. All four words in the bank are indefinites, and only chiunque fits: qualunque wants a noun, ciascuno needs a group to distribute over, and nessuno would flatly contradict the praise in the second half. The subjunctive sia stato is chiunque's doing.
examiner_note: The OIC four-way closed bank (their C1 exercise): the option set IS the distractor set, so elimination requires knowing all four words rather than just the target. Rendered as a short item with the bank stated in the prompt rather than as MCQ, because each distractor here is a genuinely distinct wrong ANSWER the guards can attribute to this bucket -- unlike ind_chi_02, where the error is additive and only index-scoring reaches it.

prompt: Complete: '____ abbia scritto questo messaggio, è stato molto gentile.' [whoever wrote]

must_not_include.note: Chi opens a relative clause with a different flavour; the free-choice 'whoever at all' word is chiunque.

explanation: Whoever wrote this message was very kind: Chiunque abbia scritto questo messaggio, è stato molto gentile. Chiunque opens the clause on its own and takes the subjunctive abbia scritto, supplied here. The writer is unknown and could be anybody, which is precisely the sense chiunque carries.
examiner_note: Criterion 18: 'Chi' is a substring of 'Chiunque' (c,h,i), so the guard MUST be word-anchored or it fires on every correct answer -- criterion 18's third direction, a guard inside a plausible correct attempt, a false diagnostic. match_at 'word' throughout. Mirrors ind_chi_01 with a different frame and a compound subjunctive (house technique 3: same word, different conditioning).
